Since the 1963 acquisition of the paneling from the Hôtel de Varengeville and the Palais Paar with funds given by Mr. and Mrs. Charles Wrightsman, these rooms have borne the Wrightsmans' name.

The Wrightsman Galleries

Devoted to the decorative arts of seventeenth- and especially eighteenth-century France, The Wrightsman Galleries (522–529, 531–533, and 545–547) display the Museum's holdings of furniture, Savonnerie carpets, gilt bronze, Sèvres porcelain, silver, and gold boxes. Since the 1963 acquisition of the paneling from the Hôtel de Varengeville and the Palais Paar with funds given by Mr. and Mrs. Charles Wrightsman, these rooms have borne the Wrightsmans' name.

The Wrightsman Galleries

The display of furniture and Sèvres porcelain in gallery 529 highlights the role of the influential marchands merciers, luxury dealers and tastemakers who established new fashions in eighteenth-century Paris.

Faience, or tin-glazed and enameled earthenware, first emerged in France during the sixteenth century and is foremost distinguished by the milky opaque white color achieved by the addition of tin oxide to the glaze. Although characterized as more provincial in style than porcelain, French faience was used at the court of Louis XIV as part of elaborate meals and displays. The term faience derives from the northern Italian city of Faenza, once a major center of maiolica production that influenced the early production of French faience. Regional variations in vessel shapes and decorative motifs gradually emerged in cities such as Nevers, Rouen, Lyon, Moustiers, and Marseille.

The Wrightsman Galleries

The objects in this gallery, dating from the reign of Louis XIV (1638–1715), are made of such precious materials as tropical woods, tortoiseshell, ivory, and gilt bronze. Known as the Sun King, Louis was one of the longest reigning and most powerful monarchs in France. With the help of Jean-Baptiste Colbert (1619–1683), the superintendent of finance, the arts of the day revolved around the king's personal taste and celebrated the magnificence and formality of his court. Royal symbols frequently served as decoration; rarely has a style been more closely associated with the personality of a ruler. Louis XIV focused his attention on transforming his father's hunting lodge at Versailles into a royal residence that became the setting for his court in 1682. The king himself is depicted as Jupiter in the embroidered hanging to the left of the bed. This lavish set of hangings was commissioned by the king's mistress, Madame de Montespan (1641–1707), about 1684–85.

The Wrightsman Galleries

Members of the French royal family and nobility are portrayed on many of the medals shown here. On the reverse of the medals are images and inscriptions celebrating the sitter's virtues or accomplishments, such as a military victory. Medals are both portable and durable and could be reproduced, making them ideal for spreading and preserving an individual's fame and achievements. Ancient Roman rulers who were admired in seventeenth-century France had used coins for this purpose. Louis XIV (1638–1715) relied on medals to promote the powerful royal image he had carefully cultivated as part of his mission to strengthen the monarchy. Also displayed in this gallery are carved hardstone cups, enameled watches, bronze statuettes, and other precious objects representative of the French nobility's taste for luxurious collectibles.

long_name: Sculpture and Decorative Arts in Central Europe, 1700–1800
description:
The Lesley and Emma Sheafer Collection in The Wrightsman Galleries

The furniture, ceramics, silver, and glass in this gallery were made in the German-speaking states of Central Europe. The extravagant curvilinear forms and exuberant natural motifs reflect a distinctly Germanic interpretation of the lively Rococo style that originated in France in the 1730s. Evoking trellises heavy with blooms, the set of carved furniture from Seehof Castle, near Bamberg, is one of the purest expressions in the decorative arts of the Rococo fascination with nature and fantasy.

Central European sculptors primarily carved wood, favoring elongated figures in rhythmic poses that were often painted. The porcelain and earthenware objects on view attest to Germany's preeminence in ceramic production during the first half of the eighteenth century. The first true European porcelain was developed at the Meissen factory, near Dresden. The pieces shown here are among Meissen's greatest achievements.

long_name: Patio from the Castle of Vélez Blanco, 1506–15
description:
Vélez Blanco Patio

Masks, vases, and birds eating berries are among the sensitively carved details adorning the windows and other architectural elements of this marble patio from the castle of Vélez Blanco in Andalusia, Spain. The decoration also features a rich array of fantastic creatures, including sphinxes, dragons, and rams with serpent tails. These motifs were inspired by the ornamental vocabulary of ancient Rome as interpreted by Italian stonecutters working in Spain during the early sixteenth century. The coats of arms of the owners—members of the powerful Mendoza family—are carved between the patio's arches. Inner courtyards were an important feature of Spanish castles during this period, providing privacy as well as access to light and fresh air.

The owner of the castle sold the patio's marble fittings in 1904. George Blumenthal purchased them in 1913 and had them installed in his Park Avenue home. The nearly two thousand blocks of marble that form the structure were reassembled at the Museum in 1964. Monumental Italian sculptures from the sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries are displayed in the patio.

long_name: The Vélez Blanco Patio: Spanish Decorative Arts, 1450-1700
description:
The objects arranged around this balcony from a great Spanish courtyard attest to the artistic diversity of the kingdoms of Spain. Rich decoration and animated forms were nurtured by contact with Islam, Italy, Northern Europe, and the Americas. By the late fourteenth century, the area around Valencia had become a leading producer of prized lusterware—ceramic vessels that derive their seductive iridescence from a glaze containing copper and silver compounds. The potters' techniques and designs, combining geometry with nature, reflect an Islamic influence that is discernible in many Spanish arts, including textiles and furniture.

Wood was the preferred material for sculpture, primarily made to stimulate religious devotion, and usually painted to enhance its realism and poignancy. Sculpted holy figures were exported throughout the Spanish colonies, including those in the New World, to promote Catholicism. The discovery of the Americas and its precious-metal mines boosted Spanish goldsmiths' work, providing a steady stream of silver for fashioning religious accessories and secular vessels, grandly adorned with masks in the ancient Roman style and other Italian Renaissance ornamental motifs.

Over the course of the sixteenth century, French royal power became increasingly centralized, and courtly etiquette grew more formal and refined. François I (1494–1547; ruled 1515–47), known to his contemporaries and to later historians as the Father of Arts and Letters, gave impetus to this trend by attracting to his court humanists and artists from Italy and other parts of Europe. Working under François and his successors at the château de Fontainebleau and elsewhere, these artists developed a new vocabulary of fantastic shapes, abstract patterns, natural motifs, and elongated figures, whose sinuous proportions were in keeping with Italian Mannerist taste but also harked back to native Gothic aesthetics. Diffused through prints, their innovations were applied to a wide range of art forms, not least among them those of the decorative arts. The excellence of the resulting works reflects a long tradition of French craftsmanship as well as the heightened demand for luxury objects from an increasingly sophisticated, largely noble clientele. Although possessing functional shapes, many of the pieces exhibited here, such as the Saint-Porchaire ware and the tin-enameled earthenware dishes, were meant exclusively for display, as their fragility and often complex narratives make clear.

The Wrightsman Galleries

This carved-oak shop front (1775–77) from the Île Saint-Louis, Paris, reveals how significant the taste for decoration evoking ancient Greece and Rome, as expressed in the Neoclassical style, had become in France. It is not known what type of merchandise was originally shown behind this shop's windows. Catering to an upscale clientele, however, the marchands merciers (dealers in luxury goods) who owned such shops displayed precious silver, snuffboxes, and mounted porcelain pieces. These objects were made especially to attract the attention of the discriminating consumer. While the marchands merciers did not execute the pieces themselves, they played a role in their design and arranged for upkeep and repair.

The Wrightsman Galleries

Devoted to the decorative arts of seventeenth- and especially eighteenth-century France, The Wrightsman Galleries (522–529, 531–533, and 545–547) display the Museum's holdings of furniture, Savonnerie carpets, gilt bronze, Sèvres porcelain, silver, and gold boxes. Since the 1963 acquisition of the paneling from the Hôtel de Varengeville and the Palais Paar with funds given by Mr. and Mrs. Charles Wrightsman, these rooms have borne the Wrightsmans' name.

The Wrightsman Galleries

Devoted to the decorative arts of seventeenth- and especially eighteenth-century France, The Wrightsman Galleries (522–529, 531–533, and 545–547) display the Museum's holdings of furniture, Savonnerie carpets, gilt bronze, Sèvres porcelain, silver, and gold boxes. Since the 1963 acquisition of the paneling from the Hôtel de Varengeville and the Palais Paar with funds given by Mr. and Mrs. Charles Wrightsman, these rooms have borne the Wrightsmans' name.

Carroll and Milton Petrie European Sculpture Court
The Henry R. Kravis Wing

The Carroll and Milton Petrie European Sculpture Court features large-scale sculpture presented chronologically from the east end to the west end of the gallery. These mythological, allegorical, and historical works highlight the range of expressive content and visual effects that can be achieved in stone and metal. The material is all-important in sculpture—affecting how light and shadow play across the surface, and furthering the ambition of the design. Carving projecting limbs in stone, for example, requires tremendous skill since the potential for breakage is great. With bronze, sculptors could create a deep, lustrous finish by applying varnishes or chemicals to the surface to form a patina. This coloration also occurs naturally over time as a result of the metal's exposure to air. Several of the works in this gallery were originally displayed in palace gardens, acting as dramatic focal points in carefully arranged natural settings.

The Iris and B. Gerald Cantor Galleries
The Henry R. Kravis Wing

This space within The Iris and B. Gerald Cantor Galleries houses four of the Museum's most popular pieces of nineteenth-century sculpture. The great Neoclassical sculptor Antonio Canova is represented by the figure of the Reclining Naiad (water nymph). The figure's pose and hairstyle recall sculpture from classical antiquity, as does the use of white marble. Marble is used to very different effect for the sculpture of Cupid with two sleeping figures. Signed by Lorenzo Bartolini, the highly detailed sculpture is a tour de force of marble carving. In contrast, Charles Cordier's two busts of African women are made of bronze embellished with enamel and onyx, reflecting a new taste for polychromed sculpture.

This gallery also displays a group of important Fabergé works on long term loan from the Matilda Geddings Grey Foundation, which includes three Imperial Easter Eggs and a Lily of the Valley Basket that is considered the most important Fabergé object in North America.
This gallery has no description.

This gallery is dominated by paintings made in eighteenth-century Venice that represent ancient Roman conquests. The action takes place both far beyond the bounds of present-day Europe—in Tunisia and Numidia, both in northern Africa—and in regions that today seem well within its borders, such as northern Italy. The paintings are juxtaposed with other works from The Met collection that offer an expanded narrative of legacies of the ancient Mediterranean world.
Tiepolo’s paintings visualize the expansion of empire, attesting to ever-shifting relations between periphery and center. Rather than take their overwhelming scale and theatrical display of might at face value, we can read them against the grain and in the context of their time: they were created at a moment of identity crisis for Venice, when a grand vision of the past compensated for the city’s political decline and the ghost of longstanding military threats from the Ottoman Empire. These paintings set the stage for stories of artists grappling with ancient legacies and often violent colonial entanglements that play out in nearby galleries and continue to resonate today.

By the 1300s, painting on panel took on renewed importance as an artistic medium in Europe. Changes in church practices led to a focus on decorating altars with paintings, giving artists a prominent space to develop novel styles and techniques of storytelling. Political events also played a role: After the 1204 sacking and looting of Constantinople, one of the most vibrant artistic centers in the world, religious icons and other artworks dating back to the ancient world were dispersed. Paintings that evaded destruction reached artists working throughout Europe, inspiring a new generation.
The artworks here reflect the great variety of Christian religious imagery produced over the course of several centuries in Europe when painting took center stage, including multipanel altarpieces, processional banners, and works that suggest images could be divinely created. Indeed, an important aspect of the history of European devotional painting can be found in Christian legend itself, in which Saint Luke is discussed as the first icon painter, and Jesus as the author of the first “true image”—an imprint of his own likeness left on a cloth used to wipe his face on the way to his crucifixion.

Before the fifteenth century, independent portraits were rare in Europe. Those that did appear—usually in sculpture, coinage, or manuscript illumination—most often pictured an idealized royal or holy figure. The proliferation of painted portraits of individuals around the year 1450 aligned with a new emphasis on personal religious belief and self-commemoration, as well as the increased spending power of merchants and bankers—all characteristics of the period historians now term the Renaissance. Artists often designed painted portraits to be portable. These works traveled across Europe as part of diplomatic and personal exchanges, accelerating contact between different artistic traditions.

Both painters and sculptors looked to portrait types from ancient Greece and Rome to depict their sitters as influential or learned. Others took advantage of the developing medium of oil paint to achieve new effects of realism. Details of wrinkles, scars, or stubble add powerful presence to painted faces, while carefully rendered textiles and jewels record status and wealth for posterity.

The Harry Payne Bingham Gallery

The influential trio of Titian, Jacopo Tintoretto, and Paolo Veronese painted both religious and secular images with heightened sensitivity and theatrical intensity. Inspired by poetic traditions, Venetian artists in the 1500s became perceptive interpreters of human emotion, exploring devotion, desire, joy, and despair. They introduced a deep intimacy to painting, championing what was then an uncommon subject—the sensuous female nude—and transforming familiar biblical narratives into introspective scenes set at night.

With dazzling color, soft contours, and light effects, painters in Venice positioned themselves in opposition to the rival tradition in Florence that focused on draftsmanship and the science of anatomy. Innovative to the point of rebelliousness, they harnessed the expressive potential of oil painting, applying paint thickly and employing a sketchy, almost vibrating style of brushwork. Their legacy would span centuries, touching the work of artists such as Peter Paul Rubens, Velázquez, Eugène Delacroix, and Edouard Manet.

The figures should stir the spectators’ souls—disturbing them in some cases, cheering them in others, in others again inciting them either to compassion or disdain, depending on the character of the subject matter. Failing this, the painter should not claim to have accomplished anything.
—Ludovico Dolce, 1557

A Venetian humanist, prolific author, and art theorist, Dolce traveled in the circles of artists such as Titian. He engaged with the artistic controversies of the day in his famous treatise on painting.

The R.H. Macy Gallery

Around 1500, religious painting in Italy underwent a major shift. The multipanel, or polyptych, format was gradually abandoned in favor of one large canvas or wood panel. This change invited a new relationship between viewers and the divine. While multipart altarpieces like those in this gallery by Raphael and Perugino could tell many stories, single-scene works prioritized the experience of a worshipper boldly coming face-to-face with the subject of devotion. Freed from compartmentalizing frames, artists transformed sacred figures into grand entities who gazed directly at viewers, conveyed palpable emotions, and at times appeared to move beyond the picture.

Public altarpieces performed several functions, including serving as the focus of Mass, educating the faithful, and honoring the patron who commissioned them. For artists, they provided prominent settings to display their work and advance their careers. As such, altarpieces were increasingly seen as vehicles for experimentation and arenas for competition. The search for novelty led to great variety, from intimate single-figure compositions to dramatic multifigure narratives that bring scripture to life.

long_name: A Search for Truth in Lombardy
description:
C. Michael Paul Gallery

Milan, the economic and political center of the Lombard region in northern Italy, hosted some of the most famed artists of the Renaissance. Ruled by the dukes of the Sforza family, the city fostered regional artistic talent and became home to Leonardo da Vinci, who left his native Florence to work at the court of Ludovico Sforza around 1482. During his roughly two decades in Milan, he influenced generations of artists.

At the heart of Lombard painting was a search for truth. In the work of Leonardo’s followers, some of whom are featured here, we find evidence of his careful observation of nature, his interest in capturing human psychology, and his technique of sfumato, a way of rendering forms through soft gradations that lend a smoky effect. Works of art from northern Europe also passed through Lombardy via trade networks and inspired an even greater interest in descriptive detail. In their stark contrasts between beauty and ugliness, humility and the divine, the painters active in this region would lay the foundation for the groundbreaking later work of Caravaggio.

Domenikos Theotokopoulos, known as El Greco (The Greek), stands alone in European painting. His idiosyncratic vision left few followers in subsequent generations, before suddenly inspiring key figures of modernism at the turn of the twentieth century, including Pablo Picasso, Paul Cézanne, and Max Beckmann.

Born on the island of Crete, then part of the Venetian empire, El Greco trained in the production of tradition-bound Byzantine icons before he moved to Italy in the late 1560s. There, he integrated into his work the contorted, elongated human bodies of Mannerist painting and the chalky, high-key palette and dynamic compositions of Venetian artists like Titian and Paolo Veronese. In 1577 El Greco moved to Toledo, Spain, where he established a workshop that created small-scale devotional works, portraits, and vast altarpieces that melded these diverse sources.

Here is an old master who is not merely modern, but actually appears a good many steps ahead of us, turning back to show us the way
—Roger Fry, 1920

Deemed incomprehensible, even aberrant, for three centuries after his death, El Greco’s paintings spoke to many artists and critics—including Roger Fry—in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries.

“Baroque” originated as a pejorative word, describing something that was overly complex and flawed in its logic—or, at its best, beautiful in the way of an irregularly shaped pearl. Bucking the staid stability of Renaissance compositions, a generation of Italian artists working around 1600 explored new approaches to narrative subjects that harnessed theatrical gestures and dramatic light effects. Instead of idealizing their figures according to classical norms, they painted the quirks of their models’ faces and physiques.

Emerging out of the expressive figure style of Mannerist painting, members of the Carracci family pioneered many of the trends that became known as Baroque. The painter Caravaggio, however, has come to embody this movement in his stormy personality, his daring use of lighting, and his unflinching depiction of unidealized bodies. In the words of the French painter Poussin—who remained adamantly classical in the wake of these developments—Caravaggio “came to destroy painting.”

In 1648 an academy of painting and sculpture was established in Paris. While its founders acknowledged the enormous significance of Italian art, from ancient times onward, they aimed to forge a new school of distinctly French painting. This institution regulated taste and professional success in the French art world well into the 1800s, often challenging the new directions taken by pioneering artists.

In its early years, the academy prioritized intellectual engagement with ancient history, claiming this set artists apart from mere craftsmen. Its founders favored works that employed the jewel tones, clarity of light, and classical decorum found in paintings by Simon Vouet and Charles Le Brun. As a royally sponsored institution, the academy aligned itself with the priorities of the absolute monarch, Louis XIV, whose palace of Versailles became a nexus for artistic activity.

An alternate thread of expression built on the vision of Caravaggio and his many followers in northern Europe, focusing on subjects that had been largely looked down on by the academy. Artists such as Georges de La Tour captured the poetic beauty of everyday life with a careful orchestration of light and restrained sense of composition.

Muslim North Africans occupied large parts of southern Spain for over seven hundred years, battling for territory with Catholic rulers, who took definitive control in 1492. For Spanish art, this resulted in profound expressions of Catholicism built atop one of Europe’s most multicultural societies.

Through marriages and political alliances, the so-called Catholic Monarchs of Spain controlled not only large portions of the Americas, but also Flanders and southern Italy. Spanish painting, therefore, took inspiration from a wide range of sources that innovatively combined regional styles, with Peter Paul Rubens and Caravaggio standing out as touchstones. Prints and foreign artwork were readily accessible in the new capital of Madrid, where Spanish kings amassed vast collections, and in Seville, the economic center of the global empire and a key artistic nexus.

The distinctive visual cultures that emerged in the Americas from the trauma of the Spanish conquest are deeply indebted to both Indigenous and European artistic traditions. They are also informed by the advent of trade across the Pacific Ocean in the late 1500s and the resulting presence of Asian art. Throughout Spanish America, painters and sculptors not only reworked imported visual models but also invented entirely new ones as they created images needed for worship, instruction, and self-presentation. Similarly, artisans fashioned new types of vessels and ornaments from locally abundant precious materials to embellish altars and adorn sacred images.

By the early seventeenth century, in response to local tastes and social practices, distinct artistic forms, styles, and genres had taken shape in far-flung locations such as Mexico City, Quito, Cuzco, and Potosí. Attracted by the visual and material qualities of local artistic traditions, European and American elites sought out aromatic earthenware vessels from Tonalá (in western Mexico); dazzling lacquerware from Pasto (in the northern Andes); richly polychromed sculpture from Guatemala; and luminous paintings on copper and inlaid mother of pearl from Mexico City.

Drue Heinz Gallery

The eighteenth century witnessed major transformations in British art, spurred by the nation’s emergence as a global economic and military power. Artists and critics began to champion a distinctly British school, and the Royal Academy of Arts, founded in 1768, conferred new prestige on painting with its annual exhibitions.

While theorists asserted the superiority of history painting, portraits dominated the market. The range of people depicted in portraiture expanded beyond the aristocracy, extending to not only the newly rich but also actresses and athletes. Other popular images catered to an urban audience’s fascination with the rural poor, sentimentalizing the inequities behind the period’s cultural flowering.

In scenes of everyday life and contemporary events, British artists responded to the transatlantic slave trade and colonial warfare, often idealizing the brutality occurring beyond Britain’s shores. At the same time, many painters from the newly independent United States relocated to London, eager to make a name for themselves in one of the world’s great artistic centers.

Early eighteenth-century visual arts reflect a cultural fascination with the supposed simplicity of country life—experienced vicariously by wealthy Parisians—and with performance, in which role reversal and masquerade unraveled social protocols. These new attitudes are encapsulated by the fête galante, a term coined to describe innovative paintings by Antoine Watteau representing “gallant” outdoor pursuits—notably amorous games imagined in romantically disheveled gardens. Musical themes suggest the lovers’ harmony and echo the amateur theater performances and concerts familiar to Watteau’s patrons. These intimate images spoke to a more informal world outside the royal court and became enormously popular; prints based on Watteau’s work and paintings by his immediate followers disseminated the fête galante across Europe.

Watteau’s world turned away from Italian grandeur and took cues from northern European painting, an often-underappreciated influence on French visual culture. Another artist attuned to Dutch and Flemish painting, Jean Siméon Chardin, pursued unassuming subject matter in still lifes and depictions of seemingly mundane daily activities. Clarity of composition, brilliance of paint handling, and focused attention counteracted the bombast of Baroque art.

Before the late sixteenth century, Europe’s artistic establishment deemed scenes from everyday life too inconsequential for the noble art of painting. Yet these subjects became increasingly popular in the market around 1650—particularly in northern Europe, where Protestant reforms encouraged artists to abandon religious art and its traditional patron, the church. Innovative contemporary scenes set in upscale townhouses and rural inns often focused on class divisions through the pointed representation of etiquette, social and family dynamics, clothing, and interior decoration. Contrasting privileged and humble settings, artists offered models for emulation but also levied biting critiques and presented figures of ridicule.

By the second quarter of the eighteenth century, two new categories of painting had developed out of these shifts. In France, tableaux de modes, or fashionable subjects, recorded the latest trends in clothing and decor; in England, “conversation pieces,” often representing families in newly casual relationships and as if caught by chance, brought a new intimacy to portraiture.

In the 1750s, art critics began accusing French painters such as François Boucher and Charles Joseph Natoire of letting the grand allegorical painting of King Louis XIV’s reign (1643–1715) disintegrate before their eyes. The art that later became known as Rococo was deemed “feminine” and “decorative,” words that continue to color the way these paintings are understood.

In fact, these artists were responding directly to changes in lifestyle by wealthy Parisians. Art patrons had left the court at Versailles for newly built urban townhouses where intimate rooms and informal entertainment reigned. Many paintings were intended to be installed into carved paneling and integrated into complex multimedia displays that required the combined skills of craftspeople who specialized in porcelain, mirrors, textiles, woodwork, gilding, and metalwork.

On the outskirts of Paris, maisons de plaisance, small retreats built specifically for entertaining, also prompted jewel-box displays that blended paintings and decorative art, indoor and outdoor. The canvases by Hubert Robert displayed here were commissioned for Bagatelle (a French word meaning “a little trifle”), the suburban retreat of King Louis XV’s brother.

long_name: Hierarchy, Gender, and the French Academy
description:
Jayne Wrightsman Gallery

Academic hierarchy structured the lives of successful French painters. Artists skilled in depicting ancient and religious history ranked highest, followed by those proficient in portraiture, then animals, then landscape, then still life. The men making these distinctions hoped to ensure that painting was taken seriously as a liberal, intellectual profession and not a craft like woodworking or metalsmithing. Ironically, an expanding exhibition culture and art market rewarded painters in a different way: the public and collectors tended to prefer to look at portraits, scenes of daily life, or animals and flowers.

Until the fall of the French monarchy in 1789, the academy permitted only four women members at any given time. This limited their voice and their ability to exhibit. Although art history has often associated women with landscape and still life, they mostly painted portraiture and history subjects. The collection at The Met affords a unique opportunity to view works by women artists of this period.

If a Painter wishes to instruct his daughter in his Art, he will never plan to make her a History Painter; he will be sure to tell her she may only pretend to the genre of portraiture, miniature or flowers. Thus he discourages her, and extinguishes the spark of her imagination. She will paint only roses; she was perhaps born to paint Heroes!
—Stéphanie-Félicité de Genlis, 1784

Weekly intellectual gatherings, typically hosted by women such as Genlis, were a dominant feature of Parisian society and created spaces for lively debate.

The artists and sitters featured in this gallery led lives fractured by political executions and exile as they rode out multiple regime changes. The statesman Talleyrand, who appears in three portraits at The Met, served successively under Louis XVI, the revolutionary government, Napoleon and, finally, the restored kings Louis XVIII and Louis Philippe. Portraits could be highly charged public statements of political alliances in such volatile times. The grand full-length format of many examples here—traditionally associated with the aristocracy and royalty—was adjusted to accommodate newly powerful individuals in society, including scientists, generals, and influential women.

France’s prosperity in the years leading to the revolution in 1789 derived significantly from its colonial territories. These included parts of India and the Caribbean, where enslaved labor ensured the profits of the tobacco, coffee, and sugar industries. Though not readily visible on the surface of every portrait, the lives of the artists and sitters here intersected with this international network through birth, politics, and economics.

Candace K. and Frederick W. Beinecke Gallery



On the heels of the American Revolution, begun in 1775, and the French Revolution, begun in 1789, a so-called Age of Revolution unfolded around the globe, from Haiti to Greece, India to Peru. Though revolutionary forces often sought freedom from colonial powers, in France the rise of Napoleon as self-appointed emperor instead initiated rapid territorial expansion. This would completely redefine the boundaries of Europe, including claims to present-day Egypt. In art and architecture, references to ancient Greek and Roman history became touchstones for legitimizing newly erected governments.

Artworks in this gallery attest to the rapid social change of this era. Women artists experienced greater opportunities; Paris was more multicultural than ever before; and “the public” became a viable entity, unified around political causes. Change was not unilaterally progressive, however. Slavery, abolished during the French Revolution, was reinstated by Napoleon in 1802. Many liberties afforded women by the revolutionary government had been retracted by 1804.

Small and portable, the paintings here speak to the many spaces where people used images for prayer. Religious works of this scale could be packed for travel, set up in homes or chapels, kept in the private cells of monks and nuns, or carried in religious processions. Artists designed many of the objects to be held in the hand or placed on a tabletop, inviting a form of worship that engaged the sense of touch as well as the eye. Their size encouraged painters to focus on individuals, forgoing densely populated dramatic stories for single-figure compositions—usually emotionally charged and aimed at stirring compassion.

These works would have been appreciated as interactive instruments of devotion, facilitating regular prayer and contemplation for people outside the walls of the church. When placed throughout the household, they also acted as reminders of the constant presence of the divine and thus the importance of maintaining virtuous behavior.

As for the reason [why we pray], it is because prayer unites you with God, makes you his familiar, he listens to you willingly, he grants you an audience at any hour and any time that you wish, and in any place where you might be.
—Sebastiano Aiello, 1594

Aiello’s writing describes the many practices of prayer that operated beyond church walls, supported by works of art like those on view here.

Three cities, each with its own distinctive architectural landmarks and civic rituals, dominated artistic production in Flanders during the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries: Bruges, Antwerp, and Brussels. In one of the most urbanized regions of Europe, these centers enjoyed great prosperity and relative autonomy that contributed to their flourishing art markets. Competition spurred innovation, encouraging the development of new modes of painting characterized by visual flamboyance and attentiveness to the details of everyday life.

In the 1400s, Bruges was the busiest port in Europe, with many international residents who patronized local artists. After its port silted up and Bruges lost its direct access to the sea, Antwerp took its place, enjoying a period of economic and artistic growth that drew astonished commentary from visitors. Brussels, meanwhile, was an important center of court culture, home to the region’s Habsburg rulers and a renowned tapestry industry.

A revolution in the subject matter of European art occurred around the year 1560. While scenes from the Bible and ancient mythology continued to enjoy the highest critical prestige, many painters in this period responded to controversies about devotional images and a growing art market by specializing in new kinds of subject matter—most notably, scenes of everyday life, often referred to as genre painting.

Artists began to depict the experiences of ordinary people in taverns or marketplaces, and to explore the morality of activities such as drinking, smoking, or conspicuous consumption. These subjects might spur viewers not only to laugh but also to reflect on their own behavior. Printmakers translated these images into engravings and reproduced them for a wide audience, creating a rich dialogue between artists north and south of the Alps.

We hardly see a beautiful hall or fine apartment of any cost that is not decorated with pictures of beggars, bordellos, taverns, tobacco smokers, musicians, dirty children on their pots, and other things more filthy and worse.
—Gerard de Lairesse, 1707

Largely forgotten today, De Lairesse was celebrated in his lifetime as a painter and advocate for an idealizing manner based on the study of classical antiquity—hence his objection to the new down-to-earth subject matter that appealed to collectors.

Constance and Walter Burke Gallery

Anchored by two masterpieces—Pieter Bruegel the Elder’s Harvesters (1565) and Nicolas Poussin’s Blind Orion Searching for the Rising Sun (1658)—this installation showcases works from the Department of European Paintings in a new light by examining how artists helped shape cultural conceptions of the natural world, and how human and nonhuman histories are intertwined. Dating from around 1525 to 1775, paintings included in this gallery trace the broad shift in European depictions of nature from expressions of embeddedness toward assertions of dominance. Individual artists’ conceptions nevertheless remained varied and complex. Summer abundance is celebrated, for example, in many of these paintings, yet they were created during a period known as the “Little Ice Age.”

The installation additionally explores how, as physical objects, paintings alter natural resources, from flax woven into linen canvases to mined minerals ground into pigments and bound with linseed or walnut oils. Reacting to changes in temperature, humidity, and light over centuries, these materials continue to have lives of their own.

Paintings executed for churches and palaces were often gigantic; they required a tremendous outlay of time and expense. Most painters made drawings that helped them work out the composition and iconography of a painting in advance, but sketches done in oil paint allowed them to resolve additional issues, including the relationship between colors and the distribution of light. Contracts for paintings frequently required the patron’s approval of an oil sketch before the artist began the final canvas or fresco. In large studios such as Peter Paul Rubens’s, oil sketches could also serve as a guide for the painter’s assistants or for the production of a composition in other media altogether, such as tapestry.

Although oil sketches were long seen as merely tools in an artist’s working process, eighteenth-century collectors came to value them in their own right. The look of the oil sketch, with its lively summary brushstrokes, in turn influenced the style of finished works by artists like Giovanni Battista Tiepolo and Jean Honoré Fragonard.

Oil paint on canvas won out as the dominant choice for European painting, but at various points in history and in different regions, artists painted on other materials: copper, wood, plaster, and stone each had their appeal and allowed for visual effects impossible on canvas. Artists often learned how best to apply pigments to new surfaces by borrowing techniques from other media, such as sculpture, ceramics, stone work, and jewelry.

Through groupings of paintings on canvas, copper, wood, plaster, and stone, this gallery offers an opportunity to compare features of some of the supports European painters used from about 1320 to 1880.

Goya’s career bridges an age confident in the powers of reason, during the 1770s, with one that suspected the world might be an irrational nightmare, by 1800. Long viewed by historians as a solitary genius, the artist is increasingly understood to have emerged from a complex artistic milieu in Spain and as the head of a significant workshop.

After studying in Rome, in 1777 Goya returned to Madrid, where he designed tapestries that earned him the royal court’s recognition. He became the king’s official painter in 1789, but skirted convention by exploring an incisive and often unflattering form of portraiture. Goya responded to the French invasion and occupation of Spain (1808–14) with artworks that reflect on the horrors of war—themes anticipated by his earlier provocative print series that had critiqued superstition, ignorance, and inherited wealth. Goya’s propensity for critique weaves subtly through the works here; his innovations in paint and politics link these galleries to those featuring nineteenth-century paintings.

This gallery contains two groups: the enormous paintings by Tiepolo that were once part of immersive interiors and a rotating selection of pastels. The production of framed oil paintings that could be easily picked up and moved was just one aspect of European painters’ careers. Many artists were also engaged in the design of theater sets, festival architecture, furniture, tapestries, and gardens, as well as commissions to decorate huge expanses of ceilings and walls in churches, palaces, and public buildings. The examples on view are by Tiepolo.

Pastels contain many of the same pigments as oil paint, bound together with gum and powder into ready-to-use sticks. Portable and not requiring a drying process, pastels allowed artists to work quickly, applying color directly to paper or vellum. The medium’s distinctive visual effects—powdery surfaces, intense colors, and lines that reveal the artist’s touch—became the calling cards for celebrity pastelists who traveled between European cities at the peak of pastel’s popularity in the eighteenth century.

Working across various European courts, the Venetian painter Giovanni Battista Tiepolo engineered enormous allegorical decorations that glorified family lineage. On view here are preparatory oil studies for some of his most important commissions, including illusionistic ceilings in Germany and Spain, alongside oil sketches by painters from Italy, England, Spain, and Hungary that demonstrate his international impact.

The Met offers one of the most comprehensive collections of Tiepolo’s work outside of Italy. Read together, these paintings also document the Italian peninsula’s multiracial composition. Black men and women appear repeatedly in his paintings, in roles ranging from the symbolic to the everyday. Black Venetians had been part of the city since at least the fifteenth century, when an international slave trade forcibly transported people from North and sub-Saharan Africa to Europe. Within their lifetimes, many individuals obtained their freedom, and their descendants practiced a wide range of professions.

Tiepolo’s career and his fame were international. In addition to making multiyear journeys to execute site-specific works in oil and fresco (indicated above in black), he also received commissions that he fulfilled remotely (indicated in white).

The Lore Heinemann Gallery

Much like tourists today, travelers in the past sought souvenirs to commemorate their time abroad. Vedute, an Italian word for topographical “views,” refers to paintings and prints that range from the documentary to the imaginary. They flourished in Rome and especially in Venice, where the painters Canaletto and Francesco Guardi produced series of works that record the city’s sites. Often purchased and hung in dense groups, as in this gallery, they functioned like luxurious snapshots that could transport their viewers and recall shared experiences on the grand tour.

Canaletto and Guardi each developed a signature style. Canaletto employed optical devices and precise perspectival drawings to suggest clarity and objectivity, presenting Venice bathed in bright, even sunlight. His exacting geometries, often made with a straightedge, can be seen tracing individual buildings and the perspective lines of his squares and canals. Guardi preferred a looser paint handling; detail is deliberately sacrificed in favor of atmospheric effects of light and weather. His large decorative paintings take inspiration from the outskirts of Venice’s marshy lagoon but include invented follies, or fanciful architecture. These additions suited a European fashion for asymmetry and ruins that became known as the “picturesque.”

The André Mertens Galleries for Musical Instruments
The John Pierpont Morgan Wing

The Met is home to one of the world’s most diverse and important collections of musical instruments. Explore the artistry, diverse forms and interwoven uses of brass instruments in the Fanfare installation. This display showcases 74 instruments spanning two millennia and five continents.

The André Mertens Galleries for Musical Instruments
The John Pierpont Morgan Wing

Music is a central part of every culture across time and place. Local musical traditions and instruments are shaped by available materials, specific technologies, and interactions with other cultures. Historically, music and instruments, language, and cuisine have accompanied people on paths forged by global trade, war, enslavement, and migration. The adaptation of musical concepts and new materials along with belief systems and political structures reflects the creative impetus of converging cultures, traditions, and history. The Mapping the Art of Music gallery explores the impact of geography on musical culture. The displays focus on the development of regional styles, methods of transmission, adaptation and assimilation of musical instruments, and the dynamics between musical change and the continuity of cultural identity.

The André Mertens Galleries for Musical Instruments
The John Pierpont Morgan Wing

The Met is home to one of the world’s most diverse and important collections of musical instruments. Through the theme of The Art of Music, the galleries explore the artistry of instrument making and music across 4,000 years of history and around the globe in the context of the Met’s encyclopedic collections.

The André Mertens Galleries for Musical Instruments
The John Pierpont Morgan Wing

The Met is home to one of the world’s most diverse and important collections of musical instruments. Our 1830 Thomas Appleton organ is one of the oldest functioning pipe organs made in the United States.

The André Mertens Galleries for Musical Instruments
The John Pierpont Morgan Wing

Music is central to nearly all aspects of human endeavor and culture. Through the theme of the art of music, these galleries explore the artistry of music and instruments across 4,000 years of history and around the globe in the diverse context of the Met’s encyclopedic collections. The Art of Music through Time gallery is organized chronologically to illustrate that people worldwide have simultaneously created extraordinary music and instruments for millennia. Such a global perspective enables us to identify underlying commonalities in the creation and function of the instruments encountered here. The use of music and instruments to express status, identity, and spirituality, and the impact of trade, changing tastes, availability of materials, and emerging technologies are shared elements that span the sweep of time and geography. Although primarily intended to be heard, instruments also function as powerful vehicles of visual expression and are often prized as works of art in their own right. As such, their appearance frequently reflects contemporary style, and the production techniques and materials used to make them are shared with other art forms. Here, related objects and paintings are displayed to illustrate the universal presence of music and instruments in art and society.

The hand-held fan was an unexpected muse for some of the most innovative artists in 19th-century Europe. Fans became hugely popular across many levels of society during this period, serving as functional and fashionable objects of adornment and communication. Well-known French Impressionists such as Edgar Degas and Camille Pissarro not only featured this feminine accessory in their work but also adopted it as an experimental format for their art.

Fanmania investigates why avant-garde artists incorporated fans into their work and sheds light on themes of gender, courtship, consumerism, and appropriation. Artists were attracted to the semicircular form for myriad reasons, including fascination with fans from Asia and Spain, commercial ambition, and their interest in formal and technical innovation. Displaying more than 75 artworks from across The Met collection, this multimedia exhibition features painted and printed fans from Europe and Asia as well as artworks that depict women wielding fans to explore the phenomenon of “fanmania.”
  
Exhibition Open: Through May 12, 2026

long_name: Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries of Eighteenth-Century American Art
description:
Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries of Eighteenth-Century American Art

This gallery has no description.

The Robert A. Ellison Jr. Collection showcases the continuum of artistic ceramics in the United States from 1876 to 1956. The earliest examples date to the Centennial Exhibition in Philadelphia, the catalyst for the emerging American art pottery movement. Ceramists absorbed styles and techniques from France and England, and from the Far East. The tradition of painted ceramics was explored in myriad ways. By the late 1890s, the Arts and Crafts movement took hold with its flat, simplified, and conventionalized nature motifs and executed mainly with matte glazes. The period saw a proliferation in glaze experiments, from the rich red of the Chinese sang de boeuf and innovative matte glazes to luster and iridescent finishes, as well as jewel-like crystalline surfaces. The 1920s through the 1950s reflected the major art movements and ultimately ushered in the era of the studio potter.

long_name: European Migration and North American Furniture, 1600-1730
description:
The Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries of Eighteenth-Century American Art

The artworks in this gallery represent the earliest forms in the North American Colonies produced by European settlers, predominantly from England, France, Germany, and the Netherlands. Their design, function, markings, and condition reveal the complexities of spirituality, social hierachies, and commerce in the 1600s and early 1700s.

North American furniture of this period was frequently made of oak with nailed or mortise-and-tenon joinery. Whether constructed using turned parts or frames and inset panels, the furniture was durable and solid with straight, sober lines. Colonial artisans in the 1600s were heavily influenced by Mannerism, a northern European interpretation of art from the late Italian Renaissance. Their designs feature stylized plant forms carved in low relief and abstract, geometric shapes. Makers applied split-turned elements and moldings to surfaces and sometimes painted them to achieve dramatic architectural depth.

The Hart Room is the earliest period room in the American Wing. Its architectural elements are typical of seventeenth-century New England interiors, and the furnishings are fine examples of late seventeenth-century Massachusetts-made furniture, a melding of late Renaissance and Mannerist designs from northern Europe and England and craft practices brought to the colonies by English joiners and turners.

The room is from the home of Samuel (1645–1725) and Sarah Norton (1647–1727) Hart of Ipswich, Massachusetts.

The Wentworth Room is from the Portsmouth, New Hampshire, home of John Wentworth (1671–1730) and his wife, Sarah Hunking Wentworth (ca. 1673–1741). The room is furnished with imported and American-made furniture in the William and Mary style.

Originally a "best chamber," or bedroom, on the second floor, the Wentworth Room is one of the largest domestic interiors surviving from the early colonial period. Period diaries reveal the best chamber was used for dining and entertaining in addition to sleeping. The room probably contained a high bedstead with a handsome set of bed hangings, but this installation has omitted the bed in order to show a variety of new furniture forms and the activities that took place in the room.

The architectural woodwork in this room demonstrates the retention of traditional Netherlandish building practices in communities of Dutch descent in late colonial New York and New Jersey. This main chamber comes from a house built in 1751 for Daniel Pieter Winne (1720–1800), whose ancestors came to Rensselaerwyck (present-day Albany County) in 1643 from Flanders via Curaçao.

Furnished as a gallery, this room includes a variety of forms of furniture, metalwork, and ceramics distinctive to New York households of Dutch heritage.

The New England meetinghouse provided a place for religious, social, and civic gatherings. The heavy timber-framed ceiling of this gallery is a 1924 reproduction of the ceiling in the 1681 meetinghouse in Hingham, Massachusetts—the last surviving example of the once-numerous seventeenth-century meetinghouses. The high, open-timbered space is based on the halls of English manor houses built in the Gothic tradition and serves to recall the late medieval heritage of the first American colonists. The large windows at the gable ends of the gallery, intended to provide adequate natural light, follow the precedent of such English halls.

The Hewlett Room is presented as a gallery of painted furniture rather than furnished as a period room. During the seventeenth century, newly instituted trade with the Far East and India introduced materials and decorative techniques previously unknown to Europe. Motifs from these popular imported wares, such as ceramics, lacquer, and textiles, found their way onto American painted furniture. Chests were often painted to simulate exotic woods, marbles, tortoiseshell, or ivory. Naturalistic landscapes and courting couples similar to those found in contemporary needlework sometimes decorated this bright and fanciful furniture.

Select pieces of eighteenth-century New York furniture are displayed in this alcove, the reinstallation of which was made possible by The Peggy N. and Roger G. Gerry Charitable Trust. The paneling on this fireplace wall is made of gumwood, which in the eighteenth century was used almost exclusively in the New York area. The Benjamin Hasbrouck House, from which the paneling was taken, still stands.

The front door of the Wentworth House opened directly onto this small but beautifully detailed staircase. The spiral turnings on the stair balusters are the earliest known in New England. The Wentworth Room (Gallery 711) was on the second story of the same house.

This room comes from a townhouse built for Henry Craig (1767–1823), a Baltimore merchant and ship owner. Although it served as the Craig family's parlor, it is here presented and furnished as a dining room in order to display examples of the adoption and adaptation of the English Neoclassical style in the early Federal period.

The Benkard Room

Many of the pieces on view in this parlor once belonged to the noted early twentieth-century collector Bertha King Benkard, for whom the room is named. It was originally part of a house built in Petersburg, Virginia, but prominently features furniture made in New York City by leading cabinetmakers such as Duncan Phyfe. Like the nearby Baltimore and Haverhill Rooms, the Benkard Room exemplifies the popularity in the young United States of the Neoclassical style evident in the work of the eighteenth-century British architects Robert and James Adam.

One of the most spectacular rooms in the American Wing, endowed in memory of Annie Laurie Aitken, the Richmond Room offers a glimpse of the grandeur of early nineteenth-century domestic life as it was lived by wealthy Americans. Purchased from a house built for the Richmond lawyer William Clayton Williams (1768–1817), the room's most notable features are its rich mahogany woodwork and blue-and-gray King of Prussia–marble baseboards. The modern wallpaper featuring scenes of Paris is a reproduction of the type sold in the United States in the 1810s. The elegant furniture by Charles-Honoré Lannuier and Duncan Phyfe further enhances the sophisticated Anglo-French aesthetic of the room.

The Haverhill Room is the formal parlor (now furnished as a bedroom) from a house that originally belonged to James Duncan Jr. (1756–1822). Its interior represents the Neoclassical style of architecture that prevailed during the early 1800s and provides an appropriate backdrop for the American Wing's strong collection of New England furniture from this era.
The Israel Sack Galleries

This room comes from the North Family Dwelling in the Shaker community of New Lebanon, New York, which functioned between 1787 and 1947. The room reveals some of the most typical characteristics of Shaker design: utility, simplicity, and beauty. It has clean, white plaster walls, a scrubbed pine floor, and simple stained woodwork that has aged to a warm ocher. It is furnished with objects that would have been found in a typical retiring room (bedroom), although none are original to it and some were made at other northeastern Shaker communities. Although this room is shown with only one bed, a room of this size would have been shared by several women.

The Worsham-Rockefeller Dressing Room is a jewel box of a room and a quintessential expression of the Aesthetic movement, which was in vogue during the late 1870s and early 1880s. The movement stressed the artistic and embraced an amalgamation of different styles, as seen here in the flat, stylized, natural ornamentation in combination with carved flourishes in the Renaissance style. The room comes from the 4 West 54th Street home of Arabella Worsham (ca. 1850–1924), mistress (and later, wife) of railroad magnate Collis P. Huntington. She commissioned George A. Schastey (American, 1839–1894) to decorate the house in 1881.
The entrance foyer to "Art of Native America," with rotating displays of Native American art.

Frank Lloyd Wright (1867–1959) can be counted among the truly great architects of the twentieth century. This installation provides Museum visitors with the only opportunity to experience a Wright-designed domestic space in New York City. It was originally the living room in the Prairie-style lake house in Wayzata, Minnesota, created for Mr. and Mrs. Francis W. Little. The room served both as a family gathering place and as an informal concert hall. It is a light-filled pavilion, in which the division between interior and exterior has been minimized. The oak furniture was all designed by Wright and is installed following a floor plan made by the architect and a photograph of the room published in 1942.

long_name: Art of Native America: The Charles and Valerie Diker Collection

long_name: The Calculated Curve: Eighteenth-Century American Furniture
description:
The George M. and Linda H. Kaufman Galleries in the Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries of Eighteenth-Century American Art


Visitors to the Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries will encounter a dramatic presentation of masterworks in The Met collection of North American furniture produced between 1720 and 1775. During this period of great aesthetic achievement, talented makers rejected the heft and rectilinearity of earlier approaches and embraced more liberating designs conceived almost entirely of curves and abstract motifs inspired by nature. This display encourages greater consideration of the works’ sculptural expression and elastic forms, the interplay of light, and the artists’ abilities to push materials to their limits.

Furniture has always been intertwined with everyday life, but it is also an essential art form. Underlining its utility are thoughtful mathematical and architectural strategies, structural engineering, and aesthetic innovations akin to other mediums. The shapely forms and textured ornament, as well as the incorporation of ergonomics to accommodate the human body, reveal the fundamental artistry of furniture design.

Some of the brilliant furniture makers featured in this installation are identified, while others’ names were not recorded. Despite their crucial contributions, talented people of color, immigrants, and teenagers were least likely to receive credit for their work. Women were also critical to the production and reception of early North American furniture, providing immeasurable operational support to workshops and access to capital and patrons through kinship networks. Additional furniture holdings—and their fascinating stories—can be explored in the historical interiors, or period rooms, throughout the American Wing.

long_name: The Calculated Curve: Eighteenth-Century American Furniture
description:
Joyce B. Cowin Gallery in the Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries of Eighteenth-Century American Art

Visitors to the Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries will encounter a dramatic presentation of masterworks in The Met collection of North American furniture produced between 1720 and 1775. During this period of great aesthetic achievement, talented makers rejected the heft and rectilinearity of earlier approaches and embraced more liberating designs conceived almost entirely of curves and abstract motifs inspired by nature. This display encourages greater consideration of the works’ sculptural expression and elastic forms, the interplay of light, and the artists’ abilities to push materials to their limits.

Furniture has always been intertwined with everyday life, but it is also an essential art form. Underlining its utility are thoughtful mathematical and architectural strategies, structural engineering, and aesthetic innovations akin to other mediums. The shapely forms and textured ornament, as well as the incorporation of ergonomics to accommodate the human body, reveal the fundamental artistry of furniture design.

Some of the brilliant furniture makers featured in this installation are identified, while others’ names were not recorded. Despite their crucial contributions, talented people of color, immigrants, and teenagers were least likely to receive credit for their work. Women were also critical to the production and reception of early North American furniture, providing immeasurable operational support to workshops and access to capital and patrons through kinship networks. Additional furniture holdings—and their fascinating stories—can be explored in the historical interiors, or period rooms, throughout the American Wing.

Van Rensselaer Hall Albany, New York, 1765–69

The Anglo-Palladian manor house built over a four-year period by Stephen Van Rensselaer II on the outskirts of Albany after his marriage to Catherine Livingston in 1764 was public confirmation that British culture had finally, a full century after the takeover of New Amsterdam in 1664, supplanted that of the Dutch in the Hudson River valley. Until then, the Van Rensselaers, among the earliest Dutch settlers of New York and long the region's largest landowners, had lived, like their tenants, in traditional Dutch farmhouses (see the New York Dutch Room). The new manor's entrance hall, the grandest and best preserved of all domestic interiors from the New York colony, proudly aped London's version of the French Rococo. The woodwork includes leafy carved panels taken from a just-published carvers' design book. The grisaille wallpaper—the decorative program includes depictions of classical Roman landscapes and the four seasons, all within elaborate frames—was hand painted in London specifically for the room. This was as close as an American colonist could get to collecting and decorating with framed European oil paintings.

long_name: Portrait Miniatures by American Women Artists
description:
Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries of Eighteenth-Century American Art

Based on earlier European models, the tradition of miniature painting—making tiny watercolor portraits on ivory—emerged in colonial America in the 1740s, when Mary Roberts painted the first known example in Charleston, South Carolina. Women were believed to be particularly well-suited for miniature painting, as the art form was commonly associated with emotion, intimacy, and a delicacy of hand and brush. Despite this gender stereotype, miniature painting offered aspiring women artists opportunities for professionalization in a male-dominated art world. Although formal artistic training remained limited in the early nineteenth century, women miniaturists such as Sarah Goodridge led the profession. After the invention of photography in 1839, many miniature painters abandoned the art form. It was subsequently revived in the early twentieth century, when women artists again picked up their brushes to paint exacting portrait miniatures.

The Use of Ivory
For thousands of years, artisans across the globe have made objects from African and Asian elephant-tusk ivory, but the beauty of these works, including miniatures, has come at a profound cost, irrevocably decimating the world’s elephant population. These historical objects are products of their times, and these inhumane practices are now restricted by laws banning trade in ivory.

The Joan Whitney Payson Galleries

This space and the nearby galleries (756 and 757) explore ideas of cultural identity, place, and time in American art and history. American identities are always shifting and expanding, informed by notions of self and community. Just as art can help promote a shared sense of values, history, and memory, artists have often revealed a nuanced understanding of identity and its construction, especially through specific depictions of gender, race, and class. In this gallery, portraiture from the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries represents a range of subjects from different cultural backgrounds, produced by both native-born and émigré artists. Taken together, the works inspire questions about the promise and challenges of a multiracial democracy.

The Joan Whitney Payson Galleries

Throughout the centuries, the varied landscapes of North America have captured the imaginations of artists—from Indigenous inhabitants of ancestral homelands to the Euro-American settlers who followed them. Working from diverse perspectives, artists have represented plains, mountains, and waterways from the Arctic to the Caribbean under different conditions of weather and light, revealing the powerful cultural significance of the scenery. Many embraced European traditions of landscape painting, first creating sketches or oil studies in nature, then translating their observations into finished studio pictures. The painting and sculpture in this gallery examine enduring themes such as human relationships to the natural world; the taming of “wilderness” through tourism, exploration, development, and industrialization; and the urge to own, preserve, or conserve limited resources. These works invite inquiry across time and place into the many meanings of the North American landscape.

Barbara and Martha Fleischman Gallery

The early to mid-1900s were a dynamic time when American artists explored what it meant to be modern. In its broadest terms, modernism—more a cultural attitude than a coherent movement—rejected past styles in order to experiment and remake the future toward positive, even utopian, ends. Many were committed to enlarging the role of art in society by creating an “art for the people.” To this end, painters and sculptors adopted a realist approach and used regional subjects to explore the importance of place and history—in addition to expressing modern themes. Shaped by the country’s increasingly international profile and its inward-looking nativism, these artists developed innovative vocabularies that emphasized form, color, and composition, sometimes influenced by European examples. The vibrancy of urban culture and the quieter symbolism of nature and handicraft informed their work as well.

The Joan Whitney Payson Galleries

Soon after the Civil War (1861–65), American artists became increasingly professionalized. They also adopted a newly self-conscious approach to their creative identities and practice. In urban centers like New York and Philadelphia, painters and sculptors established networks centered around studios, schools, museums, clubs, and commercial establishments. These cosmopolitan environments—which made up multiple overlapping art worlds—drew people with diverse backgrounds and training. Variously schooled in Europe and local academies, this emerging generation included professional women and artists of color. Embracing the cultural tenets of the American Aesthetic Movement, they experimented stylistically and across a range of media, blurring the lines between the fine and decorative arts. These artists pursued a broad range of subjects, prioritizing figure painting and scenes of modern life. Displaying savvy in marketing to a diverse clientele, they also exchanged works to honor their friendships and shared artistic values.

Richard and Maureen Chilton Gallery in the The Joan Whitney Payson Galleries

Beginning in the 1870s, Americans flocked to Europe to experience the celebrated art and culture of the continent. This exposure was transformational as art and ideas were exchanged across international boundaries. For artists, Paris—then the center of the art world—offered unparalleled training opportunities. Many sought education in other cities, including Munich, Dusseldorf, and Rome. Those who remained at home often studied at academies modeled after European precedents. Still others—such as Mary Cassatt, Frederick William MacMonnies, John Singer Sargent, Henry Ossawa Tanner, and James McNeill Whistler—spent most of their lives abroad as they cultivated successful careers on both sides of the Atlantic. During this increasingly cosmopolitan era, artists traveled widely in search of subjects for their work. In destinations such as India, Egypt, and Japan they could indulge a popular fascination with diverse cultures.

B. Gerald Cantor Sculpture Gallery

The B. Gerald Cantor Sculpture Gallery features The Met’s historic collection of bronzes, marbles, terracottas, and plasters by Auguste Rodin (1840–1917). The Museum acquired many of the works displayed here directly from the great French sculptor. Others are gifts from renowned collectors such as Iris and B. Gerald Cantor, after whom the gallery is named. All reveal Rodin’s intense exploration of the human figure as he strove, in his words, to “imitate not only form, but life.”

The paintings in this gallery provide a glimpse into the creative forces that shattered conventions and invigorated individual expression across Europe during Rodin’s lifetime. Front and center are monumental scenes that reconceive traditional subjects from history, religion, and myth in modern terms. Artists were equally inspired by the beauty and vitality of the body, the dynamism of nature, and the world of the imagination. Today, as then, the emotional eloquence and inventive modeling of Rodin’s sculptures speak to an astonishing range of pictorial subjects and styles.

Kenneth Jay Lane Gallery in the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries

In 1798 Napoleon conquered Egypt, which had been a province of the Ottoman Empire since 1517. Although he was driven out by the British after one year, his campaign unleashed in Europe a profound curiosity about North Africa and the Middle East, which was immediately made manifest in European painting.

Throughout the nineteenth century, artists crossed the straits of Gibraltar and roamed as far as Syria and Turkey in search of picturesque motifs and exotic landscapes. This gallery displays works by several generations of artists, from David's student Girodet, to Ingres's student Chassériau, to one of the last Neoclassical painters, J. L. Gérôme.

The Kenneth Jay Lane Gallery is one of ten rooms that comprise the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries.

long_name: The Rise of Plein-Air Oil Sketching and Landscape Painting about 1800
description:
Henry J. Heinz II Galleries

This gallery features small-scale works, including many painted on paper rather than canvas, that attest to the proliferation of plein-air painting among French artists working at home and in Italy about 1800. The practice of oil sketching out of doors tended to be prefatory to painting more finished compositions in the studio and eventually led to the successive innovations of the Romantics, Realists, and Impressionists.

This gallery is one of ten that comprise the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries.

long_name: Northern European Painters in Italy about 1800
description:
Henry J. Heinz II Galleries

This gallery features works by artists from throughout northern Europe who were active in Italy either as official members of arts academies or as part of more informal communities of artists, especially in and around Rome and the Bay of Naples. The selection ranges from topographical sketches to idealized landscapes and from figure studies to genre scenes that illustrate episodes from antiquity, religious themes, local customs, and brigands. Many of these paintings contain the seeds of Romanticism and Realism, even as they extend the Neoclassical tradition.

This gallery is one of ten that comprise the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries.

Christen Sveaas Gallery in the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries
Michael C. Rockefeller Wing

The Christen Sveaas Gallery, unique in the United States, displays landscapes by Caspar David Friedrich and his Romantic followers in Dresden, including Carl Gustav Carus and August Heinrich. Another colleague of Friedrich's, the Norwegian Johan Christian Dahl, is represented by major paintings depicting views in Denmark, Germany, Italy, and his native country. These works showcase Dahl's role as a figure whose artistic sources and influences were international in scope. Small-scale sketches and large pictures reflect these early-19th-century artists' experience of the Sublime in nature—whether the haunting beauty of a moonlit night, the awesome power of an alpine waterfall, or the fearsome display of an active volcano.

Intimately-scaled scenes of daily life represent the so-called Golden Age of Danish painting. The charming Biedermeier and German Realist styles are represented in works by Eduard Gaertner and Adolph Menzel, who both worked in Berlin.

The gallery features major paintings given by Christen Sveaas to The Met in 2019, in celebration of its 150th anniversary. It is one of ten rooms that comprise the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries.

Henry J. Heinz II Galleries

For much of the nineteenth century, British painting was a powerful stimulus to artists throughout Europe. In no area was Britain more influential than landscape painting. The lively brushwork, sparkling light, and realistic detail found in John Constable's scenes of the English countryside—which caused a sensation at the Paris Salon of 1824—spurred artists to depict their native scenery with new directness. J. M. W. Turner's dramatic, sometimes nearly abstract, evocations of sea, air, and sky, inspired painters as diverse as Eugène Delacroix and Claude Monet to portray the fleeting effects of nature in their work. A similar vigor and candor animates British figure painting of the period, which is often rooted in contemporary life.

This gallery is one of ten that comprise the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries.

The Havemeyer Galleries

Four galleries (Gallery 814–817) feature the Museum's collection of works by Degas, one of the world's largest and most comprehensive.

The richness of these holdings is due in large part to Mrs. Louisine Havemeyer, one of the principal collectors of modern European art in America at the turn of the twentieth century. She began acquiring Degas's work in the late 1870s at the urging of her friend, Mary Cassatt, the famous American Impressionist, and came to prefer his work above all other living artists.

By the time of her death, Mrs. Havemeyer had amassed a collection of more than sixty paintings, drawings, and pastels by Degas, as well as all seventy bronzes and a large number of prints. The bulk of her collection—the most complete group of Degas's works ever assembled—entered the Metropolitan upon Mrs. Havemeyer's death in 1929. This gallery also includes small-scale works by Tissot and other artists in Degas's circle.

This gallery is one of three that comprise the Havemeyer Galleries.

long_name: The Annenberg Collection: Nineteenth- and Twentieth-Century Masters
description:
The Annenberg Galleries

The Annenberg Galleries (Galleries 818–826) includes more than fifty works by eighteen of the most renowned artists working in France during the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, among them Manet, Monet, Renoir, Cézanne, Van Gogh, Gauguin, and Picasso.

The Annenberg Collection (Galleries 821-823) was assembled by Walter H. Annenberg (1908–2002) and his wife, Leonore Annenberg (1918–2009), over a period of four decades, beginning in the early 1950s. The Annenberg gift stipulates that the collection must be exhibited together and always remain on the premises of the Metropolitan Museum.
